_**User (2026-04-06 19:34:12Z)**_

Base directory for this skill: /Users/orenmagid/claude-cabinet/.claude/skills/orient

# /orient — Session Briefing

## Purpose

Start every session with a briefing. Before anyone makes a decision,
assemble what happened since last time, what needs attention, and what's
on the agenda. Without this, Claude starts every session blind — same
mistakes, same questions, same missed context. Orient reads the past so
debrief can write the future. That's the loop that gives your cabinet
continuity.

This is a **skeleton skill** using the `phases/` directory pattern. The
orchestration (what to do and in what order) is generic. Your project
defines the specifics — what files to read, what data to sync, what work
items to check — in phase files under `phases/`.

### Phase File Protocol

Phase files have three states:

| State | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| Absent or empty | Use this skeleton's **default behavior** for the phase |
| Contains only `skip: true` | **Explicitly opted out** — skip this phase entirely |
| Contains content | **Custom behavior** — use the file's content instead |

The skeleton always does something reasonable when a phase file is absent.
Phase files customize, not enable. Use `skip: true` when you actively
don't want a phase to run — not even the default.

## Why This Matters

If Claude Code starts a session without reading what happened last time,
it has no memory. If it ends a session without recording what happened,
the next session starts blind. Orient doesn't need to be complex — a
minimal orient reads a project description and a status file. A mature
orient pulls fresh data, checks queues, evaluates health, and surfaces
what needs attention. The complexity grows from use: each check gets
added because its absence caused a problem. But the loop itself must
exist from day one, or nothing that follows has a foundation.

## Workflow

### 1. Load Context (core)

Read `phases/context.md` for the list of files and state to load at
session start. This typically includes status files, memory from prior
sessions, and project-specific context.

**Default (absent/empty):** Read at minimum:
- The project's root `CLAUDE.md` (already loaded by Claude Code)
- `system-status.md` or equivalent state file if one exists
- `.claude/memory/patterns/` — enforcement patterns from prior sessions.
  Scan the directory, read each pattern file. These are project-level
  feedback that guides behavior (what to avoid, what to keep doing).

The goal: build a mental model of where things stand before doing
anything else.

### 2. Sync Data (core)

Read `phases/data-sync.md` for how to pull fresh canonical data from
remote sources (databases, APIs, shared storage).

**Skip (absent/empty).** Purely local projects don't need it. Projects
with remote canonical data stores define their sync commands here.

Report if sync fails — a stale local cache is better than no data, but
the user should know it's stale.

### 3. Scan Work Items (core)

Read `phases/work-scan.md` for what work items to check. This includes
whatever the project uses to track work: a backlog, task list, inbox,
queue, or issue tracker.

**Skip (absent/empty).** But note: without work scanning, orient can
only report on project state, not on what needs doing. This phase is
what connects orientation to action.

### 4. Health Checks (core)

Read `phases/health-checks.md` for system health and validation checks
to run at session start. These catch problems early — stale data, broken
references, failed background processes, configuration drift.

**Skip (absent/empty).** Projects add health checks as they discover
failure modes worth detecting early.

**Built-in check (always runs):** If `~/.claude/cc-registry.json`
exists, verify this project is in it and the entry is current. If
other registry entries point to paths that no longer exist, silently
note it — mention during briefing only if the user might care (e.g.,
"Your old project 'deal-v1' seems to have been deleted — want me to
remove it from the registry?").

> **Orient vs Pulse vs Audit:** Orient health checks verify *operational*
> state — is the system running, is data fresh, are processes alive?
> Pulse (embedded in orient) verifies *descriptive* accuracy — do counts
> match, do documented states match reality? Audit verifies *quality*
> through expert cabinet members — is the code sound, are conventions
> holding? Orient runs every session; pulse runs inside it; audit runs
> periodically. Each asks a different question about the same system.

### 5. Auto-Maintenance (core)

Read `phases/auto-maintenance.md` for recurring automated tasks that
should run every session. These are operations that would decay if left
to human memory — the anti-entropy principle in action.

**Skip (absent/empty).** Projects add maintenance tasks as they discover
operations that need regular execution but aren't worth remembering to
invoke manually.

### 6. Activate Cabinet Members (core)

Read `phases/cabinet.md` for which expert cabinet members or lenses
should be active during this session. Cabinet members watch for specific
concerns (quality, security, process adherence, non-project items)
without being explicitly invoked for each decision.

**Skip (absent/empty).**

### 7. Present Briefing (presentation)

Read `phases/briefing.md` for how to present the orientation results.
This phase controls format, sections, tone, and any time-aware or
context-aware presentation modes.

**Default (absent/empty):** Present a simple summary of what was gathered
in steps 1-6: project state, work items needing attention, any health
issues found, maintenance results.

### 8. Discover Custom Phases

After running the core phases above, check for any additional phase
files in `phases/` that the skeleton doesn't define. These are project-
specific extensions. Each custom phase file declares its position in the
workflow (e.g., "runs after work scan, before briefing"). Execute them
at their declared position.

### 9. Name the Session

Rename the session so the sidebar is scannable. Every session that starts
with `/orient` looks identical in the history — naming fixes this.

After the briefing and the user's response, derive a short name (3-6
words) from what the user says they're working on. If the user hasn't
stated a focus, ask.

## Phase Summary

| Phase | Absent = | What it customizes |
|-------|----------|-------------------|
| `context.md` | Default: read CLAUDE.md, status, memory | What files and state to load |
| `data-sync.md` | Skip | How to sync remote data |
| `work-scan.md` | Skip | What work items to check |
| `health-checks.md` | Skip | System health checks |
| `auto-maintenance.md` | Skip | Recurring session-start tasks |
| `cabinet.md` | Skip | Which cabinet members to activate |
| `briefing.md` | Default: simple summary | How to present orientation |

## Quick Mode

Phases are either **core** (maintain system state) or **presentation**
(surface information for the user). For lightweight sessions where the
user already knows what they're doing, skip presentation phases. Core
phases always run because they keep the system healthy.

- **Core phases** (always run): context, data-sync, work-scan,
  health-checks, auto-maintenance, cabinet
- **Presentation phases** (skippable): briefing

A project that wants a quick orient variant skips the briefing phase
and outputs a one-line confirmation instead.

## Extending

To customize a phase: write content in the corresponding `phases/` file.
To skip a phase: leave the file empty or don't create it.
To add a phase the skeleton doesn't define: create a new file in
`phases/` with a description of when it runs relative to the core
phases. Claude reads whatever phase files exist at runtime.

Examples of phases mature projects add:
- Command queue processing (check for instructions from external UIs)
- Deferred item evaluation (re-check trigger conditions on paused work)
- Time-aware briefing modes (first session of day vs. returning session)
- Proactive skill suggestions (surface tools relevant to current state)
- Calendar integration (upcoming events that need preparation)

## Calibration

**Core failure this targets:** Starting a session without context, forcing
the user to reconstruct state from memory or ask for information the
system already has.

### Without Skill (Bad)

New session starts. Claude says "How can I help?" The user asks about
their project status — Claude searches files, reads logs, gives a partial
picture. The user asks about pending tasks — Claude queries again. The
user mentions something from last session — Claude has no memory of it.

Three round trips to assemble context that one orientation would have
surfaced. Meanwhile, an overdue deadline sits unmentioned because nobody
asked about it.

### With Skill (Good)

New session starts. Claude loads project state, syncs fresh data, scans
the work backlog, and presents: here's where things stand, here's what
needs attention, here's what changed since last time. The overdue item
is surfaced before the user has to remember it. One message, full
picture. The user decides what to work on from an informed position.


---

1	# System Status — Claude Cabinet
2	
3	## What's Built
4	
5	- CLI installer with interactive and flag-based module selection
6	- 8 modules: session-loop, hooks, work-tracking, planning, compliance, audit, lifecycle, validate
7	- 3 install modes: Everything, Lean (`--lean`), Custom (interactive per-module)
8	- Template copying with hash-based conflict detection and manifest tracking
9	- Existing installs: add-only (no overwrite prompts), updates via `/cc-upgrade`
10	- Safe reset via `--reset` (manifest-aware, won't delete customized files)
11	- Settings merge (hooks into `.claude/settings.json`)
12	- Optional SQLite work tracker setup
13	- 20 expert cabinet members for audit system
14	- Split briefing files: identity, architecture, scopes, cabinet, work-tracking, api
15	- Cabinet member gap detection in debrief (anti-entropy)
16	- Feedback outbox for non-developers (`~/.claude/cc-feedback-outbox.json`)
17	- GitHub setup guide for non-developer feedback delivery
18	- Conversational onboard, seed, and cc-upgrade skills
19	- Link/unlink skills for local dev workflow
20	- Publish skill with post-publish dogfood sync
21	- Extract skill for proposing upstream extraction from consuming projects
22	- Upstream feedback loop: debrief phase auto-surfaces CC friction from consuming projects
23	- Write protection: hook blocks edits to manifest-tracked files, prevents downstream drift
24	- Drift detection: `cc-drift-check.cjs` compares file hashes against manifest
25	- Dogfooded: installed on itself via `--lean`
26	
27	## What's Active
28	
29	- Published at v0.6.8 on npm as `create-claude-cabinet`
30	- One downstream consumer: Flow (20 upstream + 11 project-specific cabinet members, fully migrated to v0.6.8)
31	- install.sh fetches latest version dynamically from npm (no more hardcoded version)
32	- install.sh manifest builder only tracks upstream template files (not all project files)
33	- cc-health has v0.5→v0.6 content audit (7B) and structural integrity checks (7C)
34	- cc-upgrade has legacy migration step (2.5), intelligent terminology pass, and `upgrade/` cleanup
35	- Upstream feedback loop active
36	- Write protection + drift detection active
37	- Legacy manifest detection: installer now reads `.corrc.json` for v0.5.x upgrades

39	- Two-file committee system: upstream `committees.yaml` + project `committees-project.yaml`

1	# Context Loading
2	
3	Read these files at the start of every session:
4	
5	1. `CLAUDE.md` — project rules and conventions
6	2. `system-status.md` — what's built, active, and planned
7	3. `.claude/briefing/_briefing.md` — full project briefing for cabinet members
8	
9	## Extraction Proposals
10	
11	Check both sources for pending proposals:
12	
13	1. **Local proposals** — scan `proposals/` for any `.md` files (not `.gitkeep`).
14	   These are filed by `/extract` from linked consuming projects.
15	2. **GitHub issues** — run `gh issue list --label extraction-proposal --state open`
16	   to check for proposals filed from non-linked consuming projects.
17	
18	If either source has proposals, surface them early in the briefing:
19	"There are N extraction proposals pending review" with a one-line
20	summary of each.
21	
22	## Field Feedback
23	
24	Check both sources for incoming friction reports from consuming projects:
25	
26	1. **Local feedback** — scan `feedback/` for any `.md` files.
27	   These are filed automatically during debrief in linked consuming projects.
28	2. **GitHub issues** — run `gh issue list --label field-feedback --state open`
29	   to check for feedback from non-linked consuming projects.
30	
31	If either source has feedback, surface it in the briefing:
32	"There are N field feedback reports from consuming projects" with
33	the component name and a one-line summary of the friction for each.
34	
35	Field feedback is different from extraction proposals: proposals suggest
36	artifacts to upstream, feedback reports friction with existing CoR
37	components. Both are valuable — proposals expand, feedback refines.
38
```
</details></tool-use>

Perfect. Now let me summarize all the findings:

## Summary

I've found and reviewed all the requested files. Here's what exists:

### Phase Files

**Location:** `/Users/orenmagid/claude-cabinet/.claude/skills/orient/phases/`

Only **1 phase file exists**:
- `context.md` (1.5 KB)

**Contents of context.md:**
This file documents the context loading process for sessions. It specifies that at the start of every session, three files should be read:
1. `CLAUDE.md` (project rules and conventions)
2. `system-status.md` (what's built, active, and planned)
3. `.claude/briefing/_briefing.md` (full project briefing)

It also describes two feedback mechanisms:
- **Extraction Proposals:** Check `proposals/` directory and GitHub issues with `extraction-proposal` label for artifacts to upstream
- **Field Feedback:** Check `feedback/` directory and GitHub issues with `field-feedback` label for friction reports from consuming projects

### Other Configuration Files

**system-status.md** (exists at `/Users/orenmagid/claude-cabinet/system-status.md`):
- **What's Built:** 8 modules, 3 install modes, template copying, settings merge, 20 expert cabinet members, split briefing files, feedback outbox, multiple skills (link/unlink, publish, extract, etc.), write protection and drift detection
- **What's Active:** Published at v0.6.8 on npm, one downstream consumer (Flow project), upstream feedback loop active, legacy migration support
- **What's Planned:** Extract 11 Flow cabinet members, build general migration, archive old repo, onboard testing

**cc-registry.json** (exists at `/Users/orenmagid/.claude/cc-registry.json`):
Registry of 3 projects:
1. `claude-cabinet` (v0.6.6) — the main source repo
2. `multiShopper` (v0.5.4) — experimental project
3. `flow` (v0.6.7) — downstream consumer

### Memory/Patterns Directory

**Does NOT exist:** `/Users/orenmagid/.claude/memory/patterns/` — this directory has not been created yet.

There is a `.claude/memory/` directory structure elsewhere for specific projects (e.g., under `.claude/projects/`), but no general patterns directory.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-06 19:34:48Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./.claude/briefing/_briefing.md`

```text
1	# Cabinet Briefing — Claude Cabinet
2	
3	## What This Project Is
4	
5	Claude Cabinet (CC) is an npm package (`create-claude-cabinet`) that scaffolds
6	opinionated process infrastructure for Claude Code projects. It's a Node CLI
7	that copies skill templates, hooks, and scripts into a target project's `.claude/`
8	directory, then uses a conversational `/onboard` skill to generate project-specific
9	briefings. The audience is anyone using Claude Code — developers and non-developers
10	alike. The codebase is small: ~6 JS files in `lib/`, templates in `templates/`.
11	
12	## Core Principles
13	
14	1. **Intelligence is the merge strategy.** CC distributes as conversation, not
15	   config. Upgrades, onboarding, and adaptation happen through Claude reasoning
16	   about context — not mechanical diffing.
17	2. **Phase files customize, not enable.** Skills always do something reasonable
18	   when phase files are absent. Phase files override defaults; `skip: true`
19	   disables them. No config files, no YAML, no DSL.
20	3. **Session continuity over session intelligence.** The core value is orient/debrief:
21	   Claude starts informed, ends by recording what happened. Everything else is opt-in.
22	4. **Meet people where they are.** The system works for senior engineers and
23	   first-time coders. Onboarding listens to how you talk and matches your level.
24	5. **Minimal footprint.** Nothing touches source code. Everything lives in
25	   `.claude/`, `scripts/`, or root metadata files.
26	
27	## Architecture
28	
29	Single-layer Node CLI with no build step:
30	
31	- `bin/create-claude-cabinet.js` — entry point
32	- `lib/cli.js` — argument parsing, module selection, orchestration
33	- `lib/copy.js` — template file copying with conflict detection
34	- `lib/metadata.js` — `.ccrc.json` read/write (manifest, module state)
35	- `lib/settings-merge.js` — merges hook config into `.claude/settings.json`
36	- `lib/db-setup.js` — optional SQLite work tracker setup
37	- `lib/reset.js` — safe removal using manifest hashes
38	- `install.sh` — shell installer (no Node.js required, downloads from npm registry)
39	- `templates/` — all skill definitions, phase files, hooks, scripts, cabinet members
40	
41	No data store. No server. One runtime dependency (`prompts` for interactive CLI).
42	Two install paths: `npx create-claude-cabinet` (Node.js) or `curl | bash` (shell).
43	Published to npm as `create-claude-cabinet`.
44	
45	## The Cabinet
46	
47	All 20 generic cabinet members are installed. Most relevant for this project:
48	
49	- **architecture** — CLI structure, module boundaries, template/runtime separation
50	- **workflow-cop** — the meta-process layer (CC's own session loop, onboarding flow)
51	- **record-keeper** — README accuracy, skill SKILL.md quality, phase file clarity
52	- **usability** — CLI UX, onboard interview quality, error messages
53	- **roster-check** — whether the skill set covers the project's actual needs
54	- **anti-confirmation** — challenge assumptions about the methodology itself
55	
56	### Portfolio Rules
57	
58	Standard portfolio rules apply. Architecture stays in code structure, workflow-cop stays
59	in workflow, record-keeper stays in content quality.
60	
61	### How Cabinet Members Are Invoked
62	
63	Via `/audit` for full sweeps, `/pulse` for quick checks, or composed into
64	`/plan` critique. The audit module is installed (lean install).
65	
66	## User Context
67	
68	Oren Magid — creator and product owner of Claude Cabinet. Self-describes as
69	"the idea man, not the architect." Relies on Claude for implementation,
70	architectural decisions, and planning. Deep context on the methodology's
71	purpose and philosophy; delegates technical execution.
72	
73	## Paths
74	
75	### App Source
76	`lib/*.js` — all CLI logic (6 files)
77	`bin/create-claude-cabinet.js` — entry point
78	
79	### Templates
80	`templates/skills/` — all skill definitions and phase files
81	`templates/hooks/` — hook scripts
82	`templates/scripts/` — database and triage tooling
83	`templates/rules/` — compliance templates
84	`templates/memory/` — pattern templates
85	
86	### Documentation Files
87	`README.md` — public-facing install instructions, feature list, philosophy.
88	Must stay in sync with: current version number, module count, cabinet member
89	count, install methods (shell + npm), CLI flags, and what actually ships.
90	
91	`system-status.md` — what's built, what's active, what's planned.
92	Must reflect reality after every session (debrief updates it).
93	
94	`CLAUDE.md` — project conventions for Claude. Must match actual codebase
95	structure and current workflow.
96	
97	`templates/skills/*/SKILL.md` — skill definitions shipped to consumers.
98	Must accurately describe their own workflow steps, phase file protocol,
99	frontmatter fields, and related file paths.
100	
101	`templates/skills/*/phases/*.md` — phase files shipped to consumers.
102	Instruction-bearing phases must be accurate. Example/template phases
103	(with commented-out content) must show realistic patterns.
104	
105	`templates/briefing/_briefing-template.md` — the template consuming projects
106	use to build their `_briefing.md`. Must list all `§` section names that
107	generic cabinet member SKILL.md files reference.
108	
109	`install.sh` — shell installer. Must download the current version,
110	install the same modules as `--lean`, and produce a working setup.
111	
112	`package.json` — version must match the latest published tag.
113	
114	### Documentation Staleness Signals
115	The most common documentation failures in this project:
116	- README claims (cabinet member count, module count, features) drifting
117	  from what actually ships in templates/
118	- system-status.md not updated after a session's work
119	- Cabinet member SKILL.md files referencing `_briefing.md §` sections that
120	  the `_briefing-template.md` doesn't define
121	- install.sh hardcoded version falling behind the latest npm publish
122	- CLAUDE.md listing files or conventions that no longer exist
123	
124	## Entity Types
125	
126	- **modules** (runtime) — named feature bundles selected during install
127	  (session-loop, hooks, work-tracking, planning, compliance, audit, lifecycle, validate)
128	- **skills** (filesystem) — markdown-defined capabilities in `.claude/skills/`
129	- **phase files** (filesystem) — customization points within skills
130	- **cabinet members** (filesystem) — expert analysis lenses in `.claude/skills/cabinet-*/`
131	- **manifest** (`.ccrc.json`) — tracks installed files and their hashes
132	
133	## Work Tracking
134	
135	No formal work tracking system (lean install skipped work-tracking module).
136	Project state tracked in `system-status.md`.
137	
138	### Hooks
139	`templates/hooks/git-guardrails.sh` — blocks destructive git ops on main
140	`templates/hooks/cc-upstream-guard.sh` — blocks writes to manifest-tracked files
141	`templates/hooks/skill-telemetry.sh` — logs skill invocations via JSONL
142	`templates/hooks/skill-tool-telemetry.sh` — logs Skill tool use via JSONL
143	`templates/hooks/stop-hook.md` — prompt hook reminding to run /debrief
144	
145	### System Status
146	`system-status.md` — what's built, what's active, what's planned.
147	
148	### Friction Captures
149	No dedicated directory yet. Field feedback from consuming projects arrives
150	in `feedback/` (from linked projects) or as GitHub issues labeled
151	`field-feedback` (from unlinked projects).
152	
153	## Codebase Layout
154	
155	```
156	lib/
157	  cli.js            — main orchestration, module definitions, arg parsing
158	  copy.js           — file copying with hash-based conflict detection
159	  metadata.js       — .ccrc.json CRUD
160	  settings-merge.js — .claude/settings.json hook merging
161	  db-setup.js       — SQLite setup (optional module)
162	  reset.js          — manifest-safe file removal
163	
164	templates/
165	  skills/           — orient, debrief, plan, execute, audit, onboard, etc.
166	  cabinet/          — committees, lifecycle, composition patterns, eval protocol
167	  briefing/         — briefing templates for consuming projects
168	  hooks/            — git-guardrails, upstream-guard, telemetry scripts
169	  scripts/          — pib-db.js, triage tools, cc-drift-check.cjs
170	  rules/            — enforcement-pipeline.md
171	  memory/           — pattern templates
172	
173	bin/
174	  create-claude-cabinet.js — npm bin entry point
175	
176	install.sh          — shell installer (no Node.js required)
177	```
178	
179	## Finding Format
180	
181	```yaml
182	finding:
183	  cabinet-member: member-name
184	  severity: critical | significant | minor | informational
185	  category: what domain this falls under
186	  description: what was found
187	  evidence: specific file:line or observation
188	  recommendation: what to do about it
189	```
190
```
</details></tool-use>
